entries.title: Can Pakistan-administered Kashmir elections calm tensions?
entries.summary: Pakistan-administered Kashmir heads to the polls after weeks of deadly protests over seats reserved for displaced people from India-administered Kashmir. One local group has called for the election to be boycotted.
entries.content:
Pakistan

Can Pakistan-administered Kashmir elections calm tensions?

July 24, 2026

Pakistan-administered Kashmir heads to the polls after weeks of deadly protests over seats reserved for displaced people from India-administered Kashmir. One local group has called for the election to be boycotted.

The first phase of local elections in Pakistan-administered Kashmir is scheduled for Monday, July 27, amid calls for a boycott from the recently outlawed Jammu Kashmir Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC), a grassroots alliance of civil society groups.

The JAAC has been demanding the abolition of 12 seats in the regional assembly reserved for people displaced from India-administered Kashmir and living in Pakistan. It argues that only residents of Pakistan-administered Kashmir should be eligible to contest regional elections.

The dispute escalated after the Supreme Court of Pakistan-administered Kashmir ruled that the refugee seats are constitutionally protected and cannot be abolished without a constitutional amendment.

The regional government outlawed the JAAC in June under anti-terrorism laws, citing threats to public order, and detained dozens of its supporters.

The ban came amid rising tensions over the refugee seats and ahead of violent protests sparked by the group's campaign to scrap them.

The unrest has left around 30 people dead since June, according to local estimates cited by Reuters news agency.

India and Pakistan both claim Kashmir in its entirety, but each country only controls a section of the territory, making the Muslim-majority region a longstanding flashpoint between the nuclear-armed neighbors since their independence from Britain in 1947.

Protests cast shadow over vote

Since early June, Pakistan-administered Kashmir — known locally as Azad Jammu and Kashmir (AJK) — has witnessed weeks of protests and sit-ins. While the dispute over the refugee seats has become a major flashpoint, JAAC has also campaigned for broader economic and governance reforms.

Following the unrest, authorities split polling into three phases, with voting in Mirpur set for July 27 and elections in the protest-hit Poonch division postponed until August 10.

Voting in the regional capital Muzaffarabad has been delayed until August 2 over security concerns. The JAAC continues to stage a sit-in in Rawalakot, near the Line of Control with India.

Demand for fair representation

As the region heads to the polls, a major question is whether the elections can restore public confidence or merely offer a temporary pause in public anger.

"We are just asking for fair representation, which is our right," Ali Raza, a 22-year-old student protester in Rawalakot told DW. "The local people who live here should have the right to choose their representatives, not the refugees who settled here from India."

"The election is a major opportunity for the people of AJK to choose their representatives," said Shazia Marri, the central information secretary of the Pakistan People's Party (PPP), which currently rules the region. "The people want their rights protected, their issues addressed, and a government that delivers on its promises."

However, JAAC leaders argue that elections alone cannot resolve the issues that brought thousands of people onto the streets.

"Our movement is not replacing one government with another over time; we need our rights, but there are just political slogans in the campaigns," one JAAC official told DW under condition of anonymity.

Imran Khan, a geopolitical expert and former director of the US Institute of Peace Pakistan, expects the movement to continue post-elections.

"While elections in AJK are important on their own merit and for the continuity of the political process, they will not address the political grievances and demands of the people who are supporting JAAC," Khan told DW. "The protest is outside the assembly. They no longer trust those institutions fully."

Has JAAC changed Kashmir's political landscape?

Analysts say the JAAC protests marked a significant shift in Pakistan-administered Kashmir's politics by bringing together citizens across party lines around economic and governance issues rather than traditional political rivalries.

For analyst Khan, JAAC is a dominant political force to reckon with in quite significant parts of Pakistan-administered Kashmir.

"They have more street credibility than mainstream politicians and parties despite the allegations of foreign backing and funding. For now, they have disrupted the status quo politics in AJK," Khan said, noting that the Pakistani establishment has the means and methods to break up such political movements.

Experts caution that rebuilding trust will require more than campaign promises.

"[The] next government in AJK will be very weak, as it will be sandwiched between pressure from both the Pakistani establishment that controls AJK through administrative and security framework and the sentiment on the street, which is very pro JAAC," argued Khan.

He added that the next government would have "little credibility and limited room to govern unless it reaches an understanding with JAAC to ease political tensions."

But politician Marri believes that trust is rebuilt by listening to people's concerns, delivering practical solutions, and empowering elected institutions.

Will protests return if reforms stall?

If key grievances remain unresolved, analysts warn that another wave of protests cannot be ruled out.

"It is too early to say that more waves of protests will be seen, but JAAC has the potential to become a lasting political force," said Khan, who suggested the group risks being weakened by internal divisions and pressure from the establishment.

"For now, both sides are treading carefully, with JAAC relying on mass support and the establishment avoiding a full crackdown while buying time," Khan added.

While the vote will determine who governs Pakistan-administered Kashmir, it is unlikely to resolve the broader constitutional dispute over Kashmir or eliminate long-standing frustrations with the region's governance.

"Governments must listen to public concerns and address them through dialogue and effective governance. Stability comes from listening to people, respecting the law, and delivering results," Marri added.

For Raza, the Gen Z protester, "The world has changed, and we have seen governments toppling through student protests in the neighboring countries; we won't stop, and protests will continue until our rights have not been given to us."

Edited by: Keith Walker

entries.title: Why Iran's Pickaxe Mountain is a tough target for the US
entries.summary: Donald Trump has threatened to attack a suspected Iranian nuclear site buried deep under a mountain. Iran's Pickaxe Mountain remains one of the biggest unanswered questions surrounding the country's nuclear program.
entries.content:
Iran

Why Iran's Pickaxe Mountain is a tough target for the US

July 24, 2026

Donald Trump has threatened to attack a suspected Iranian nuclear site buried deep under a mountain. Iran's Pickaxe Mountain remains one of the biggest unanswered questions surrounding the country's nuclear program.

A mysterious underground complex near Iran's Natanz nuclear facility is drawing global attention after US President Donald Trump suggested on Tuesday it could become the target of a military strike, with Trump claiming there may be uranium centrifuges located at the site.

"We'll be hitting that area very probably pretty soon. And there's not a thing they can do about it," Trump said in remarks to reporters at the White House.

Iran has threatened to "expand" the war if the US targets nuclear facilities.

  

Tehran has released almost no information about the complex burrowed under what is known in Farsi as "Kuh-e Kolang Gaz La," or Pickaxe Mountain.

Inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) have never been allowed inside, and much of what is known comes from satellite imagery and intelligence assessments rather than direct observation. 

 

Although the site has surfaced repeatedly in reports over the past few years, its purpose remains unclear. That uncertainty has made it one of the most closely watched locations linked to Iran's nuclear activities.

What do we know about Pickaxe Mountain?

The underground complex is located south of Tehran in Iran's Isfahan province, excavated inside a mountain about 2 kilometers (1.24 miles) from the Natanz uranium enrichment plant, one of the country's most important nuclear facilities. 

Commercial satellite images, including those published by Maxar Technologies, have shown continuous construction since 2020.

They show new tunnel entrances, access roads, security installations and large amounts of excavated rock. 

 

Construction appears to have accelerated after an explosion damaged Iran's advanced centrifuge assembly workshop at Natanz in July 2020. Iranian authorities described the blast as an act of sabotage. 

 

Months later, Tehran announced plans for a new underground facility where advanced centrifuges could be assembled in a more secure location. Officials said the replacement site would be larger, more advanced and better protected than the one that had been damaged. 

The Washington-based Institute for Science and International Security estimates that parts of the facility may be buried 80 to 100 meters (330 feet) beneath the mountain. 

 

If accurate, the complex would be deeper than many of Iran's known nuclear sites and considerably more difficult to damage with an air strike. 

IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi has repeatedly said that agency inspectors have never been granted access to the site. Without inspections, the agency cannot verify whether nuclear material, centrifuges or other sensitive equipment are present.

Reports of centrifuges in the Pickaxe complex

In June 2026, The Wall Street Journal reported that Israeli intelligence has assessed that Iran may have transferred "thousands of uranium-enrichment centrifuges" components or other sensitive equipment to the complex in the months following the 12-day bombing campaign from the US and Israel in June 2025.

Trump was asked about the report on Tuesday, and said that Iran "may have" moved the centrifuges to Pickaxe Mountain, but "we don't have it on record."

Some analysts also believe the complex could eventually support the production or assembly of advanced centrifuges if Iran decides to restore or expand those capabilities.

Iran has consistently argued that underground facilities are necessary to protect its nuclear infrastructure from sabotage and military attack. Tehran has always claimed its nuclear enrichment is for peaceful purposes and denies trying to produce a weapon.

International inspectors and intelligence reports confirm that Iran possesses approximately 970 pounds (440 kg) of uranium enriched up to 60% purity, which is far above the level needed for civilian purposes and approaches the 90% needed to produce weapons.

Bomb-proof bunker?

Farzin Nadimi, a senior fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, told DW that the strategic importance of Pickaxe Mountain "lies in its resistance to bunker-buster bombs and its proximity to the Natanz nuclear complex." 

 

Nadimi argues that the reported damage to Iran's facilities at Fordo and parts of Natanz and Isfahan after June 2025 bombing campaign has made the underground complex more important than before. 

 

"Given Iran's security considerations, one should expect that future uranium enrichment and even weapon-related activities could eventually be concentrated in a single, heavily protected location," he said. "Pickaxe Mountain is the most logical candidate for that role."

The bunker-buster bombs used by the US military are designed to penetrate terrain before detonating, but even the most powerful of those weapons can only reach a certain depth.

Whether they could destroy a complex buried as deeply as Pickaxe Mountain remains a matter of debate among military experts. The exact layout of the tunnels, their depth and the location of any critical infrastructure have never been made public.

Chance of crippling the Pickaxe facility

Unlike Natanz or Fordo, where key facilities have been identified over the years, it is difficult to assess damage to a target located inside a mountain.

 

Some analysts argue that if the site were ever targeted the objective might not be to destroy the entire complex. A more realistic military goal could be to collapse tunnel entrances, damage access routes or disable supporting infrastructure, making the facility difficult or impossible to operate. 

Whether Pickaxe Mountain eventually becomes a centrifuge production site, an enrichment facility or simply a heavily protected support complex remains unclear.

Its secrecy, location and apparent scale have turned it into one of the most closely watched pieces of Iran's nuclear program, which is likely to remain under close international scrutiny for years to come.

Edited by: Wesley Rahn

entries.title: Germany news: Top court rules out mass admission revocations
entries.summary: An Afghan woman has won victory over Germany's Interior Ministry after it cancelled her admission. Elsewhere, consumer confidence and VW profits are down, while violent extremist crime is up. Follow DW for the latest.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany news: Top court rules out mass admission revocations

Published July 24, 2026last updated July 24, 2026

An Afghan woman has won victory over Germany's Interior Ministry after it cancelled her admission. Elsewhere, consumer confidence and VW profits are down, while violent extremist crime is up. Follow DW for the latest.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Germany's Constitutional Court ruled that Afghan admission pledges cannot be revoked en masse
  • German consumer confidence is forecast to drop again in August
  • German automaker VW reports that year-on-year second quarter net profits were down by 32.9%
  • Extremist violence in the southwestern state of Baden-Württemberg saw a sharp rise across the political spectrum​​​​

Here were some of the top headlines from and about Germany on Friday, July 24, 2026:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Consumer confidence dips slightly as fuel costs rise again

Consumer confidence dips slightly as fuel costs rise again

German consumer confidence looking ahead to August has dipped slightly, according to the NIM index.

The Nuremberg Institution for Market Decisions (NIM) on Friday moved its confidence index down 0.3 points, to negative 29.6 points.

It attributed its latest slight decrease in confidence to lower expected incomes and a higher tendency to save money.

"The ongoing uncertainty is leading to many households remaining reticent when it comes to consumption," NIM expert Rolf Bürkl said. Most people anticipated negative overall economic growth in the coming 12 months, he said. 

It also noted the role of rising fuel costs, particularly diesel and gasoline, after the government's temporary tax relief expired at the beginning of July, only for fighting between the US and Iran to flare up once again.

"Given the expiry of the petrol discount at the end of June, prices at the pumps have risen considerably once again, which is reflected in the price expectations of consumers," the institute said.

The current figures are based on interviews with roughly 2,000 people conducted between July 2 and 13 — which was before the renewed fighting really kicked into gear in the Gulf and started pushing energy prices up at pace.

Skip next section Constitutional Court says Afghan admission pledges cannot be revoked en masse

Constitutional Court says Afghan admission pledges cannot be revoked en masse

Germany's Constitutional Court on Friday ruled in favor of an Afghan woman who filed a lawsuit against the federal Interior Ministry after the German government reneged on the promiseto provide protection from the Taliban on human rights grounds. 

The woman was among hundreds of individuals promised admission to Germany in 2021 after the repressive Islamist Taliban returned to power.

In December 2025, Germany's Interior Ministry reversed policy by announcing the blanket cancellation of promised admissions for roughly 640 Afghans whom the prior administration had left stranded in Pakistan for years.

The court on Friday said admissions must be considered on a case-by-case basis, adding, "Even where the executive enjoys a broad measure of discretion, it is never completely free."

Once a person has been informed that they will be granted admission, the court said, individual circumstances must be reviewed before it can be canceled.

The court said the Interior Ministry's decision to simply revoke all admissions was in breach of a German constitutional ban on arbitrary state action.

The case will now be reexamined by Germany's Higher Administrative Court.

The Constitutional Court further ordered the Interior Ministry to continue supporting those Afghans currently in Pakistan until they are admitted to Germany or the ministry reviews their cases in compliance with Germany's Basic Law.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Guten Tag from the DW newsroom. Although it's almost the weekend, smack in the middle of what used to be Germany's slowest news season, there is plenty to report.

Beyond the fact that the men's national football team has a new coach and the chancellor is reshuffling his Cabinet, we are keeping an eye on a host of other stories, including a Constitutional Court decision in favor of an Afghan woman who sued the Interior Ministry over its mass cancellation of admissions to the country.

The woman and her two young children were among hundreds of Afghans promised admission to Germany on humanitarian grounds. The court said Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt's 2025 blanket policy reversal was unconstitutional and ordered Berlin to review cases individually.   

Although the German economy has shown signs of life despite prior pessimism, the US war in Iran and resulting price hikes — on top of inflation and the adverse impact of more than four years of war in Ukraine — have again put the crunch on consumer confidence, which is expected to drop in August.

Economic bright spots came from the construction industry, which is apparently booming, as well as unexpectedly rosier sales at luxury sports car manufacturer Porsche. Volkswagen, however, complained that it only netted €1.54 billion ($1.75 billion) in second quarter profits, and says it hopes to have a cost-cutting package figured out by year's end.

If that's not enough, Baden-Württemberg's Interior Ministry registered a "worrying" rise in the number of extremist violent crimes committed in the well-heeled southwestern state, with a growing number of these — from right-wing, left-wing and Islamist fanatics — being carried out by men under the age of 30.

Stay tuned to DW for the latest in Germany news this Friday, July 24, 2026

entries.title: Turkey: What chances does Ozel's new opposition party have?
entries.summary: After being removed as head of Turkey's main opposition CHP in May, Ozgur Ozel has announced the formation of a new party. Dozens of lawmakers have followed him, but will he be able to challenge President Erdogan?
entries.content:
Turkey

Turkey: What chances does Ozel's new opposition party have?

July 24, 2026

After being removed as head of Turkey's main opposition CHP in May, Ozgur Ozel has announced the formation of a new party. Dozens of lawmakers have followed him, but will he be able to challenge President Erdogan?

In the early morning of May 24, shocking images of Ankara were broadcast around the world. After a court ordered that Ozel be ousted as head of the center-left Republican People's Party (CHP), the country's largest opposition party, its headquarters in the Turkish capital were surrounded by special forces.

The party leader and his allies had barricaded the doors with tables and chairs, but hours later they stopped resisting. "We want to avoid an escalation and are leaving of our own free will," a visibly shaken Ozel said. "Our only crime is to have defeated the ruling party."

Indeed, under his leadership, the CHP had managed something it had not succeeded in doing in 22 years. It had defeated the ruling Justice and Development Party (AKP) in a nationwide election.

In the local elections of March 2024, the party emerged as the strongest and took over control of city councils in 14 major cities. That included Istanbul, whose mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu, quickly became so popular that he established himself as a serious challenger to Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan in the next presidential election, which is scheduled for 2028.

But soon Imamoglu was arrested, alongside several other mayors and their staff. They are accused of corruption and fraud. And then in May, Ozel was removed from his position.

Two months later, he announced the formation of the Yeni Parti, or "New Party," submitting formal registration to the interior ministry. With 91 lawmakers, it is set to become the second largest party behind the AKP in Turkey's 600-seat parliament.

CHP, a divided opposition party

Founded in 1923 by the founder of the Republic of Turkey, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, the CHP is Turkey's oldest political party, standing for the separation of church and state, patriotism and republicanism.

Ozel, who came from the pro-reform faction and became head in 2023, was able to open up the party to voters beyond its core base, placing more emphasis on the values of social democracy. He met with success, as the election results showed.

But after Ozel was ousted, Kemal Kilicdaroglu, the old party leader under whose watch the CHP had lost every election for two decades, returned. Kilicdaroglu was considered to be Erdogan's preferred opponent.

Why did the CHP split?

There has been much speculation as to why the party accepted the court ruling and agreed to the party being divided. Experts believe that behind the scenes, political calculations that benefit Erdogan are at play. The split will allow him to eliminate dangerous opponents ahead of the next presidential election.

The conflict within the CHP dates back to 2023: After the defeat of Kilicdaroglu, who was party leader at the time, by Erdogan in the presidential election, the reformist faction led by Ozel and Imamoglu pressed the 75-year-old to step down. They prevailed at the party convention in the autumn of 2023.

But in May 2026, a court declared the party convention null and void due to alleged irregularities. As a result, Ozel and other leaders were removed from their posts, and Kilicdaroglu and his allies returned.

What Ozel said upon leaving CHP

Ozel was less willing to accept the ruling; he and his faction fought to the very end to remain, but they were forced out because the country's judiciary has become increasingly less independent under Erdogan.

In his farewell speech on Tuesday, Ozel called on his supporters to follow him: "We will walk together on a new path," he said. "We will march to power. Let's walk friends, let's walk friends!"

And follow him they did. The mass exodus to Ozel's Yeni Parti will leave the CHP with 44 seats in parliament, making the CHP the fourth largest in Turkey.

Can Ozel be a serious challenge to Erdogan?

Seren Selvin Korkmaz, the director of the Istanbul-based think tank IstanPol, said that Ozel's Yeni Parti had good chances of succeeding, considering it had so much support from former CHP supporters.

However, she warned against taking voters for granted. She said that a recent poll the think tank had conducted showed voters did not just want the CHP with a different name but a party that built upon its legacy while also moving beyond it.

According to the IstanPol poll, around 30% of respondents would vote for the new party or at least consider it. Another 19% said their decision would depend on the party's program, candidates and leaders.

She concluded that there was a "broad but conditional potential" for support of almost 49%, explaining that this was fueled both by sympathy for Ozel as well as hostility toward the judiciary interfering in party politics. She said that generally opposition voters were open to a new party.

Korkmaz suggested that the new party could make inroads among supporters of the nationalist conservative IYI party and the pro-Kurdish DEM, as well as certain voters of the ruling AKP party and some of the supporters of its coalition allies from the ultranationalist MHP. She added that the biggest challenge would be to unite the at times conflicting expectations of these different segments under a common umbrella.

She predicted that the party could be successful if it was able to avoid being too ideologically limited or bland and centrist. He said that it was clear from Ozel's rhetoric that he wanted to establish an inclusive center, with a republican and socio-democratic backbone.

It remains to be seen whether Ozel will be able to establish party structures in at least 41 of Turkey's 81 provinces by the time of the next presidential election in 2028. This is a prerequisite for being eligible to run.

Ozel's Yeni Parti does not have access to the same funds as the CHP, which will make the process even more difficult.

This article was originally published in German.

entries.title: Why Trump's 'forced labor' tariffs leave the world puzzled
entries.summary: After the US Supreme Court struck down Donald Trump's emergency tariffs, the White House has turned to another trade law linked to alleged human rights abuses.
entries.content:
July 24, 2026

After the US Supreme Court struck down Donald Trump's emergency tariffs, the White House has turned to another trade law linked to alleged human rights abuses.

Which countries will face Trump's new tariffs?

The Trump administration on Thursday announced new tariffs on 60 economies, including the European Union, citing their handling of imported goods made with forced labor.

In a statement, the United States Trade Representative (USTR) said it had investigated major trading partners and found that they failed to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods made through modern slavery.

The tariffs cover more than 99% of US imports and replace a temporary 10% global tariff that was due to expire.

A 10% tariff will apply to countries like Argentina, India, Pakistan and the UK that have adopted or committed to forced-labor import restrictions.

The EU, Taiwan, Japan, South Korea and Switzerland will face tariffs of either 10% or 12.5%, depending on the product.

Goods from 38 other countries will be subject to the higher tariff of 12.5%, including China, most of the Middle East, much of Latin America, Australia, New Zealand and Russia.

Exemptions include steel, aluminum, automobiles, civilian aircraft, rare earth minerals and goods covered by the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

The new tariffs took effect just after midnight Eastern Standard Time on Friday, July 24.

Are Trump's new tariffs really about forced labor?

The USTR noted that the US has banned imports made with forced labor for almost a century, adding that despite international consensus, "the prevalence of forced labor persists worldwide and has even escalated in recent years."

Washington argues that foreign producers gain an unfair price advantage from the practice and that US workers cannot compete with forced-labor wages.

In reality, many critics and analysts see the move as a legal workaround for US President Donald Trump's broad tariff program, announced on "Liberation Day," April 2, 2025, which the US Supreme Court struck down in February.

At that time, Trump invoked the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977 to impose the tariffs without congressional approval, but the court ruled that the law does not authorize presidents to impose tariffs on their own.

The new forced labor tariffs were introduced under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, a more established trade law that many analysts believe will be harder for courts to overturn.

Does the EU really have a problem with forced labor?

Compared with parts of Africa, Asia and Latin America, forced labor isn't a major issue within the 27-member EU, due to comprehensive labor laws and strong enforcement.

The International Labour Organization (ILO) defines forced labor as work performed under the threat of penalty and without voluntary consent.

While labor protections in the EU are comparatively strong, many imports still contain raw materials or components linked to forced labor elsewhere.

In December 2027, Brussels will introduce one of the world's strictest import bans, when no product tainted by forced labor will be allowed to enter, circulate or leave the EU market.

Reacting to the new tariffs, the EU's top diplomat, Kaja Kallas, compared the bloc's labor laws to those of the United States, noting that EU workers have paid vacations and other better working conditions.

Why do critics see the tariffs as a legal workaround?

Some analysts argued that the new tariffs don't keep to the spirit of the Supreme Court ruling and could be challenged.

"These new tariffs would represent another case of presidential overreach," Alan Wolff, a former deputy director-general of the World Trade Organization (WTO), wrote in a blog post ahead of the announcement.

"The world has become accustomed to higher US tariffs. There is no evidence that changing the US rationale for them will materially reduce forced labor in other countries," Wolff added.

Others said Trump was using forced labor concerns as a pretext, when the share of imports linked to forced labor from most advanced economies is negligible compared with China.

"The tariffs use real concerns about China's labor practices not as the basis for tariffs against China, but for broad tariffs against most of the world," Brad Setser, a senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations think tank, wrote on X.

Chad Bown, a senior fellow at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, also thinks the tariffs "divert US and allied attention from the trade war we should be fighting," which he said was cutting reliance on Chinese goods and building "resilient alternative supply chains."

Trade partners echoed those criticisms, with Brazil calling the tariffs "unjustified" and Australia labeling them "absurd."

Could Trump use Section 301 for more tariffs?

The administration is already using Section 301 for another investigation into excess manufacturing capacity in 16 major trading partners, including China and the EU. Trade experts expect it could lead to additional tariffs.

During his first term, the same legislation allowed Trump to impose tariffs on China over intellectual property theft and technology transfer. These were expanded under the Biden administration.

Trump's team continues to maintain existing national-security tariffs on steel, aluminum, automobiles and other products, under Section 232 of the same Act.

Trump's first administration imposed 25% duties on steel and 10% on aluminum in 2018, after the US Commerce Department found the low-cost imports threatened US national interests.

During his second term, Trump restored, expanded and increased many of those rates, while adding new tariffs on vehicles, auto parts and copper.

Edited by: Tim Rooks

entries.title: Ousted Turkish opposition leader forms second-largest party
entries.summary: With 90 lawmakers defecting alongside him, Ozgur Ozel's New Party has automatically become the second-largest group in Turkey's parliament. He leaves behind deep devisions and judicial challenges against the CHP.
entries.content:
Turkey

Ousted Turkish opposition leader forms second-largest party

July 24, 2026

With 90 lawmakers defecting alongside him, Ozgur Ozel's New Party has automatically become the second-largest group in Turkey's parliament. He leaves behind deep devisions and judicial challenges against the CHP.

The ousted leader of Turkey's opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), along with 90 other lawmakers, broke away under a new banner on Friday.

Ozgur Ozel founded the Yeni Parti, or "New Party," and has vowed to lead the fight against Turkey's longtime President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

"May it be beneficial to our country, our nation, our party, and all of us," Ozel said.

New party becomes second-biggest in parliament

With 91 lawmakers in total, the New Party becomes the second-largest party in Turkey's 600-seat parliament, after Erdogan's Justice and Development Party (AKP) which holds 277 seats.

Meanwhile, the CHP had until now been the second-largest with 135 seats, but Friday's exodus leaves the party with just 44 lawmakers — including some Ozel backers among them.

That relegates it to fourth place, behind the pro-Kurdish opposition DEM party, which has 56 seats.

"The new party we are about to establish will become the main opposition force in the Grand National Assembly by a wide margin," Ozel told lawmakers on Tuesday.

The CHP was established in 1923 by Mustafa Kemal Ataturk, who founded modern Turkey as a secular republic and served as the country’s first president.

The Kemalist party was the main challenger to the ruling conservative AKP, which has been in power for six consecutive terms since 2002.

CHP mired by division, legal troubles

Ozel's breakaway party was founded after deep divisions within the CHP.

He had won the CHP's leadership election in 2023 and reversed the party's fortunes with major gains at municipal elections the following year.

In 2025, Ozel gained an even higher profile when he became the face of mass protests to free Istanbul mayor and CHP presidential candidate Ekrem Imamoglu, who faces a prison sentence in excess of 2,000 years on charges of corruption and terrorism. He was widely seen as viable challenger to Erdogan's two-decade rule.

However, in May of this year, a court annulled the election over allegations of vote buying. The court stripped Ozel of his title and appointed his unpopular rival, Kemal Kilicdaroglu, in his place.

Critics have described the legal challenges against Imamoglu, Ozel, the CHP and its supporters as politically motivated and undemocratic.

The government denies these claims and maintains that the judiciary is independent.

After the New Party filed formation papers with the Interior Ministry on Friday, defecting Ankara MP Murat Emir told reporters: "We are leaving an occupied party behind."

Edited by: Saim Dušan Inayatullah

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Fighting wildfires is getting harder — these tips could help
entries.summary: With Spain declaring a national emergency, France seeking EU assistance and Canada dealing with more than 900 hot spots, wildfires are out of control. How can we stop our forests from burning?
entries.content:
July 24, 2026

With Spain declaring a national emergency, France seeking EU assistance and Canada dealing with more than 900 hot spots, wildfires are out of control. How can we stop our forests from burning?

Intense, relentless heat waves and prolonged drought have triggered another severe wildfire season across Europe this summer, with crews battling massive blazes in France, Spain, Portugal, Italy, the UK and even Norway.

Similar conditions have also fueled fires in the United States and Canada. For weeks, hundreds of wildfires have ripped throughout the vast boreal forest stretching from the Yukon in the north to Newfoundland in the east. As of July 24, they've wiped out at least 3.3 million hectares (8.1 million acres — an area larger than Belgium.

As toxic smoke shrouded major cities, also in the US, President Donald Trump accused Canada on social media of "not properly maintaining their Forests" and deliberately neglecting "basic Forest Management and Debris Removal."

'No one-size-fits-all' solution

While eliminating flammable fuel is part of the job for fire experts, effective management goes beyond just clearing debris. And in Canada, clearing brush from such an extensive, remote area would be impossible. "There is no one-size-fits-all" solution, said Amy Duchelle, a senior forestry officer with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).

"In forest and fire management, you have to work with the ecosystem type that you have by understanding its fire history," said Sophie Wilkinson, an assistant professor and wildfire expert at Simon Fraser University in British Columbia. "There are many different ecosystem types, such as upland forests and wetlands, and that each of those has a unique relationship with fire."

But even though Canada's boreal forests have adapted to live with fire, Wilkinson said frequent extreme heat waves and extended droughts linked to human-caused climate change are drying out trees and undergrowth, "making managing wildfires infinitely more difficult." High-intensity fires more likely to "spread more rapidly and burn more aggressively, both of which hinder management options and efficacy" — even in places previously too humid to burn.

Tropical forests like the Amazon region or Southeast Asia didn't evolve to coexist with fire — no thick, resistant bark or plants suited to germinate after a blaze. Here, it's crucial to stop deforestation linked to mining and agriculture, preserve the canopy and vegetation that creates the humid, fire-resistant habitat, and train people to immediately respond to any fire risk.

"Conversely, fragmented forests in Europe may not be fire-adapted, but may have other challenges [that weaken forests] such as invasive species," said Wilkinson. In Europe, management solutions can include cross-border monitoring and protection, and prioritizing fire-resistant vegetation instead of highly flammable conifers. That also means bringing back diverse tree species that contribute to more resilient forests, which have dwindled due to farming and exploitation over the centuries.

Fighting fire with fire

In some places, fire itself is key to preserving forests.

"Our boreal forest is a fire-dependent forest — it needs fire for health," said Amy Cardinal Christianson, an Indigenous Metis fire specialist in western Canada. She said a certain number of blazes are part of a natural cycle, creating natural firebreaks, stimulating fresh plant growth and restoring ecosystems — all of which can slow the spread of wildfires.

Research has shown that certain forests have evolved to live with periodic fires, which act as a sort of reset. The flames can clear the way for smaller plants struggling to compete with unchecked undergrowth, for example, and burned material also releases nutrients that enrich the soil.

But Canada's wildfire season is now accelerating the normal, healthy cycle — starting earlier, lasting longer and becoming harder to control.

It's also adding to Canada's carbon emissions: the 2023 record fire season, which wiped out five times the annual average of forestland, added 23% to the world's annual wildfire emissions. Some of the pollutants even made it as far as western Europe.

Christianson, a former research scientist with the Canadian Forest Service and now director of fire stewardship with the Indigenous Leadership Initiative, said older forest management practices ideas introduced by Europeans to support the timber industry — strict regulation, complete suppression and a focus on fast-growing monocultures — don't fit into the Canadian context.

"By excluding fire, we've actually accelerated the risk," she said, adding that periodic controlled burns can actually help reduce the risk of out-of-control infernos.

Global fire hub aims to share knowledge

Duchelle told DW how skilled pros who've dealt with forest fires for decades are now seeing blazes even they can't control — "the fire is moving too fast, the flames are too high, the heat is too intense."

Faced with these challenges, the focus has shifted from suppressing fires to reducing risk, preparing for changing conditions and building post-fire resilience. In 2023, the FAO set up the Global Fire Management Hub to help countries confronting wildfires to train and work together, sharing their knowledge.

A major focus is using new technology and data to direct monitoring and response, along with a digital platform to share information, and developing international interoperability and community-based management.

Christianson has worked with the Fire Hub to develop a network focused on Indigenous knowledge and practices, after setting up an independent network of more than 200 local guardians in Canada to help manage wildfire response.

That includes cultural burning — small, low-intensity deliberate blazes adapted to specific landscapes which help eliminate decaying organic matter in a controlled manner, protect wildfire and biodiversity and preserve local cultures.

Prescribed burns, which are generally done by government agencies, are also beneficial and have been employed for years. But they tend to be larger, and don't always take the local environment into account. They may also be done in the summer when conditions are drier, to speed up the process, increasing the risk of escape.

Cultural burning usually happens in early spring or late fall, based on snow and other environmental factors. But climate change has also shifted that schedule, and Christianson said it helps to be familiar with the landscape to notice those changes. "We usually would burn in April or May," she said of her property. "This year, I burned a bunch in February."

'Healthy landscapes' key to living with fire

Speaking with DW from her home in Rocky Mountain House, Alberta, Christianson said while the same burning practices can't always be applied across diverse cultures, they've still been able to share proven ideas and techniques internationally.

"The really cool thing about Indigenous cultural burning practices is that there's a lot of overarching similarities, a responsibility that we all feel to steward the landscape, and that fire is one of those important ways that we can do that," said Christianson, who has also introduced the practice to her daughters.

"The overall goal for most Indigenous folks that I've talked to is achieving healthy landscapes."

That means looking at the bigger picture — restoring wetlands to make an ecosystem more resilient in the face of fires, or reintroducing native species like the bison in Canada, which once numbered in the millions and help control fires by grazing on vegetation encroaching on grasslands and forests.

The practice has also recently been reintroduced in Europe, with horses and European bison in Spain and Portugal.

"A healthy landscape […] can more easily adapt to these fire events that we're seeing," said Christianson.

Amazon forest fires at historic low in 2025

One country that has seen recent success in tackling forest fires is Brazil.

"Brazil's been quite effective in bringing down not only their deforestation rate, but also the incidence of fires in the Amazon," said Duchelle, crediting cooperation between state, municipal and federal agencies and a national policy on integrated fire management. "[This success] didn't happen by accident."

In 2025, forest fires across South America's largest nation hit a historic low, burning around 12.7 million hectares, an area roughly the size of Greece.

New data from environmental monitoring network MapBiomas showed just under a quarter of the total burned area was in the Amazon rainforest, the world's largest and a key regulator of global climate. That's an 80% drop compared with 2024, and around 56% lower than the 40-year historical average.

The Cerrado savanna in central Brazil, however, where fire is part of the ecosystem, continued to burn at similar rates, due in part to prolonged drought. Experts have warned that the incoming El Nino weather pattern could bring even drier weather, amplifying the fire risk.

"While treating our forests can help us adapt to the rise in fire activity, addressing the root cause of that rise — climate change — is likely the best mitigation strategy available to us," said Wilkinson.

Edited by: Sarah Steffen

entries.title: How vanilla's history is not so … 'vanilla'
entries.summary: Long before "vanilla" meant bland, the bean's fandom spanned Mesoamerican elites to European courts. But it owes its ubiquity to a 12-year-old, enslaved boy.

entries.title: Klopp named new head coach of Germany men's football team
entries.summary: Germany has turned to Jürgen Klopp to lead a new era for the four-time world champions after their disappointing World Cup exit, with hopes of building toward future international tournaments.
entries.content:
Germany

Klopp named new head coach of Germany men's football team

July 24, 2026

Germany has turned to Jürgen Klopp to lead a new era for the four-time world champions after their disappointing World Cup exit, with hopes of building toward future international tournaments.

The German Football Association (DFB) has appointed Jürgen Klopp as the new head coach of the national team on Friday, ending speculation about who would succeed Julian Nagelsmann.

DFB President Bernd Neuendorf said the association's governing bodies had unanimously approved Klopp's appointment, describing him as the "ideal choice" for the role.

"It is a great honor to sit here today," Klopp said at the press conference announcing his appointment, adding he was "delighted."

What else do we know about Klopp's appointment as Germany head coach?

Klopp emerged as the federation's preferred candidate after Nagelsmann stepped down following Germany's penalty shootout defeat to Paraguay in the World Cup round of 32.

In a statement released by the DFB, Klopp was quoted as saying: "The national team can unite us Germans like almost nothing else. That is precisely what makes this role so special to me."

He said he was looking forward "to this special challenge in German football, which we will tackle together with humility and patience." He wanted to build a team "that fights for one another, that enjoys playing football, and behind which the people of our country can rally with complete conviction."

The former Liverpool manager held talks with DFB officials in New York while working as a television analyst during the tournament, and the federation announced on July 11 that the two sides had reached agreement on the essential terms of a contract.

His contract starts on August 15 and continues until the summer of 2030, with the next World Cup tournament.

His first match as Germany head coach will be against the Netherlands in Amsterdam on September 24 in Germany's opening Nations League fixture.

This is Klopp's first coaching job since he left Liverpool in 2024 citing fatigue.

The DFB also announced on Friday that Per Mertesacker will be its managing director, after his predecessor Andreas Rettig steps down at the end of the year.

Klopp's star-studded coaching career

After spending much of his career as a player for Mainz, Klopp as coach took the club to the Bundesliga for the first time.

Then, as Borussia Dortmund's coach, he took the team to back-to-back Bundesliga titles, taking the club to the Champion's League final in 2013.

His more recent coaching career saw him shine with England's Liverpool, which he coached for nine years, delivering a first league title in more than a quarter of a century and glory in the 2019 Champions League final.

Klopp was awarded the highest civic award in Germany as a result of his outstanding contribution to democracy and his impact on the football world. He was credited with becoming a role model, a football ambassador and someone who has improved the image of Germany around the world, particularly  in the United Kingdom.

Edited by: Saim Inayatullah

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Jürgen Klopp and Germany: A partnership long in the making
entries.summary: In one of the worst-kept secrets in football, the former Dortmund and Liverpool boss has been named head coach of the German men's national team.
